does: This paper introduces Do-Not-Answer, the first openly available dataset specifically designed to evaluate safety mechanisms in large language models. The dataset consists of curated instructions that responsible models should refuse to follow, spanning various harmful categories. The authors annotate responses from six popular LLMs to these dangerous prompts and train small BERT-like classifiers as safety evaluators. They demonstrate that these lightweight classifiers achieve performance comparable to GPT-4 on automatic safety evaluation tasks, providing an efficient alternative to larger proprietary models for assessing LLM safeguards.
connects: This work builds on growing efforts to evaluate and mitigate harmful outputs from LLMs, following earlier work on identifying jailbreaks and adversarial prompts targeting language models. It extends prior research on safety benchmarks by providing the first curated open-source dataset explicitly focused on dangerous capabilities that models should refuse. The paper responds to the challenge of evaluating safeguards raised by recent work on emergent harmful behaviors in scaled models, and connects to broader research on alignment and responsible AI deployment that requires systematic evaluation methodology.
enables: This dataset and evaluation framework enable future work in developing more robust safety mechanisms and fine-tuning approaches for LLMs. The availability of open-source safety classifiers allows researchers and developers to rapidly prototype and test new alignment techniques without reliance on proprietary models. The work points toward building automated safety evaluation pipelines that can scale across model development, and provides infrastructure for studying which types of harmful instructions prove most challenging for safeguards. It also enables comparative safety analysis across model families and versions, supporting the broader goal of responsible open-source LLM development.
